cluster_name: DOJ drops New York Times subpoenas over Air Force One reporting
tldr: All eleven headlines across nine country buckets treat the story as a factual withdrawal of subpoenas, with no outlet framing the government's position favorably. The main differentiation is actor labeling: English-language institutional outlets name the DOJ or Justice Department, while German, Canadian (Globe and Mail), and all three Latin American outlets name the Trump administration or Gobierno/Governo de Trump explicitly — and Brazil's Folha de São Paulo alone encodes political defeat in the verb, using 'recua' (backs down) rather than the neutral 'withdraws' or 'drops' used everywhere else.
event_summary: The U.S. DOJ on July 23 withdrew subpoenas against three New York Times reporters that sought disclosure of sources for articles on security concerns with President Trump's Qatari-gifted Air Force One jet, after a federal judge called the legal approach sloppy.

evidence.quote: after judge calls it 'sloppy legal work'
compelling 3 NYT reporters to identify sources
DOJ drops New York Times subpoenas

frames.sub_frame: Trump admin named as political actor backing down

evidence.quote: Governo Trump recua e retira intimações
Trump administration drops New York Times subpoenas

frames.sub_frame: Air Force One security flaws as named context

evidence.quote: fallas en sistemas de seguridad del avión

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only headline to use a politically evaluative verb — 'recua' (backs down/retreats) — rather than the neutral 'withdraws' or 'drops' used by every other outlet, encoding political defeat directly in the verb choice.

Unique phrase 'after admitting legal errors' characterizes the withdrawal as a confessed procedural failure rather than a discretionary choice — the strongest concessive framing in any English-language headline.

Only headline to foreground the judge's rebuke as the explicit cause ('after judge calls it sloppy legal work'), making the judicial assessment — not the DOJ's own decision — the subject of the headline.

Only headline in the corpus to surface the underlying security concern, explicitly naming 'fallas en sistemas de seguridad del avión presidencial' — all other outlets reduce the original reporting to 'Air Force One' or 'Trump plane,' stripping the security angle.

Only headline to describe the subpoenas' purpose as 'compelling 3 NYT reporters to identify sources,' naming the journalist source-protection concern as the stated object of the legal action rather than just the topic of the underlying reporting.

bottom_line: Headline coverage delivers a uniform verdict — the subpoenas were dropped — while actor labeling splits the corpus: institutional outlets in the US, India, and Southeast Asia name the DOJ or Justice Department, signaling a procedural event, while Latin American and German outlets name Trump or the Trump administration, signaling a political retreat; only Brazil's Folha encodes that retreat in its verb, and only Colombia's El Tiempo surfaces the Air Force One security concern that motivated the original reporting.
